html:
[203]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 召大将二人 - Literally 'summon two generals,' but in this context, it likely means 'call in two senior officers' or 'invite two high-ranking officials.'
  • 烦君二人救三军百姓也 - A polite request, meaning 'trouble you two to save the troops and the people.'
  • 苟利军州，死不足辞 - A formal oath, meaning 'if it benefits the army and the state, death is not worth mentioning.'
  • 君可辱为健步 - A humble way of saying 'you may serve as messengers' or 'you may undertake this task.'
  • 识其所灭处 - Means 'to recognize where they disappear' or 'to identify the place where they vanish.'
  • 垒石标表志焉 - Means 'to pile stones as a marker' or 'to set up a stone monument as a sign.'
  • 具畚锸 - Means 'to prepare shovels and spades' or 'to bring tools for digging.'
  • 偕往其所 - Means 'to go together to the place' or 'to accompany them to the site.'
  • 因发冢 - Means 'to open the tomb' or 'to excavate the grave.'
  • 获陈粟数十万斛 - Means 'to find stored grain in the tens of thousands of measures' or 'to discover a large quantity of grain.'
  • 人竟不之测 - Means 'people could not understand it' or 'no one could fathom it.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 贾相公 - Likely refers to a high-ranking official named Jia, possibly a chancellor or prime minister.
  • 滑州 - An ancient Chinese administrative region, likely referring to a specific area during the Tang or Song dynasty.
  • 大旱 - Refers to a severe drought, a common natural disaster in ancient China.
  • 秋稼尽损 - Means 'all the autumn crops were destroyed,' indicating the severity of the drought.
  • 健步 - Refers to messengers or runners, often used in military contexts.
  • 惨绯 - A dark red color, often used in formal or ceremonial attire.
  • 蕃步鬣长 - Describes a horse with long, flowing mane and tail, indicating a fine steed.
  • 大冢 - Refers to a large tomb or burial mound.
  • 畚锸 - Tools used for digging, consisting of shovels and spades.
  • 陈粟 - Refers to stored grain, often used to describe provisions or reserves.
  • 斛 - A unit of measurement for grain, equivalent to about 10 liters.
  • 人竟不之测 - Reflects the mystery and unexplained nature of the event, common in historical and legendary accounts.

Proper Nouns

  • 贾相公 - The name of a high-ranking official, likely a chancellor or prime minister.
  • 滑州 - An ancient Chinese administrative region.
  • 乙日 - A specific day, likely referring to a fixed date or a day of the week.
  • 惨绯 - A specific color, dark red.
  • 蕃步鬣长 - Describes a specific type of horse.
  • 大冢 - A specific location, a large tomb or burial mound.
  • 陈粟 - Refers to stored grain, often used to describe provisions or reserves.
  • 斛 - A unit of measurement for grain.

[204]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 胡向 - Historical figure, likely a government official or scholar during the Tang or Song dynasty.
  • 為虢州 - To serve as the governor of Guo Prefecture.
  • 時獵人殺得鹿 - At that time, a hunter killed a deer.
  • 重一百八十斤 - Weighing one hundred and eighty pounds.
  • 蹄下貫銅 - A copper ring pierced through the hoof.
  • 上有篆字 - The ring has seal script characters.
  • 博物不能識之 - Learned scholars could not identify it.
  • 博士丘濡說 - Scholar Qiu Ru said.
  • 汝州旁縣 - Nearby counties of Ruzhou Prefecture.
  • 五十年前 - Fifty years ago.
  • 村人失其女 - A villager lost his daughter.
  • 數歲忽自歸 - After several years, she suddenly returned on her own.
  • 言初被物寐中牽去 - She said she was taken away by something while asleep.
  • 倏止一处 - Suddenly stopped at one place.
  • 及明 - When it became light.
  • 乃在古塔中 - Then she was inside an ancient tower.
  • 見美丈夫 - She saw a handsome man.
  • 謂曰 - He said to her.
  • 我天人 - I am a heavenly being.
  • 分合得汝為妻 - Destiny has brought you to be my wife.
  • 自有年限 - There is a fixed term.
  • 勿生疑惧 - Do not have doubt or fear.
  • 且戒其不窥外也 - And warned her not to look outside.
  • 日两返 - Daily, he returned twice.
  • 下取食 - To get food.
  • 有时炙饵犹热 - Sometimes the roasted food was still hot.
  • 经年 - For a year.
  • 女伺其去 - The woman waited for his departure.
  • 窃窥之 - She secretly looked at it.
  • 见其腾空如飞 - She saw him soar into the air like flying.
  • 火发蓝肤 - Flaming hair and blue skin.
  • 磔磔耳如驴焉 - Cracking sound, ears like a donkey.
  • 至地乃复人矣 - When he landed, he returned to human form.
  • 惊怖汗洽 - Frightened and covered in sweat.
  • 其物返 - The creature returned.
  • 觉曰 - Realized and said.
  • 尔固窥我 - You surely spied on me.
  • 我实野叉 - I am indeed a wild demon.
  • 与尔有缘 - Fate-bound with you.
  • 终不害尔 - Finally, not to harm you.
  • 女素惠 - The woman is always kind.
  • 谢曰 - Apologized and said.
  • 我既为君妻 - I am already your wife.
  • 岂有恶乎 - How could there be evil?
  • 君既灵异 - You are already supernatural.
  • 何不居人间 - Why not live among humans?
  • 使我时见父母乎 - Let me sometimes see my parents?
  • 其物言 - The creature said.
  • 我辈罪业 - Our sins.
  • 或与人杂处 - If we live with people.
  • 则疫疠作 - Then an epidemic will happen.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 胡向 - Historical figure, likely a government official or scholar during the Tang or Song dynasty.
  • 虢州 - An administrative region in ancient China, located in what is now Henan Province.

[205]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 形迹已露 - Tracks have been revealed, meaning the situation or identity has been exposed.
  • 任公踪观 - Master Ren's observation, where '任公' likely refers to a person named Ren or a title.
  • 不久当尔归也 - You will soon return, indicating a prediction of departure.
  • 去人居止 - Distance from human residence, meaning the tower is close to human dwellings.
  • 化形 - Change form, meaning the object cannot transform into a human or another form.
  • 至地方与人杂 - Until it reaches a place where it mixes with people, meaning the object interacts with humans in public spaces.
  • 白衣尘中 - Ordinary people in the dust, likely a metaphor for common folk.
  • 敛手侧避 - Gathering hands and sidestepping, meaning the object avoids or respects certain people.
  • 枕其头唾其面 - Putting their head down and spitting on their face, likely a metaphor for disrespect or abuse.
  • 悉若不见 - All as if they did not see, meaning the object ignores such treatment.
  • 缘已尽 - The缘分 (fate) is exhausted, meaning the time for separation has come.
  • 候风雨送尔归 - Awaiting wind and rain to send you back, a poetic way of saying the object will depart.
  • 磨石饮之 - Grind the stone and drink it, referring to consuming the stone powder.
  • 下物如青泥斗余 - The substance expelled was like green mud, several dou in quantity, describing the effect of the stone.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 任公 - Likely refers to a person named Ren or a title, possibly a historical or literary figure.
  • 青石 - A talisman or magical stone, often associated with protective or transformative properties in Chinese folklore.
  • 释氏 - Refers to Buddhism, indicating a reference to Buddhist concepts or practices.
  • 风雨 - Symbolizes change or the end of a phase, often used metaphorically in Chinese literature.

Proper Nouns

  • 任公 - A proper noun referring to a person or title, likely historical or literary.
  • 青石 - A proper noun referring to the magical stone.
  • 释氏 - A proper noun referring to Buddhism.

[206]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 大历中 - Period of the Tang Dynasty known as 'Dali' (766-779 AD).
  • 请假归 - To take leave and return home.
  • 忽值引骑呵辟 - Suddenly encountered leading horses shouting and clearing a path.
  • 书吏遽映大树窥之 - Clerk hurriedly hid behind a tree to peek at it.
  • 且怪此无尊官也 - And found strange that there is no high-ranking official here.
  • 紫衣，仪卫如节使 - Wearing a purple robe, ceremonial guards like an envoy or commissioner.
  • 车勾索断 - Broken rope used to pull a carriage.
  • 捡簿 - To check the records.
  • 合取庐州某里张某妻脊筋 - Match and take the spinal cord of his wife from a certain neighborhood in Luzhou.
  • 姨尚无恙 - Aunt still unharmed.
  • 经宿忽患背疼 - After a night, suddenly suffered from back pain.
  • 元和初 - Early years of the Yuanhe era (806-820 AD) during the Tang Dynasty.
  • 因醉卧厅中 - Because drunk, fell asleep in the hall.
  • 及醒 - When he woke up.
  • 古屏上妇人等 - Women and others on an ancient screen.
  • 悉于床前踏歌 - All danced and sang in front of the bed.
  • 长安女儿踏春阳 - Young women from Xi'an stepping on the spring sunshine.
  • 无处春阳不断肠 - Nowhere the spring sunshine does not break one's heart.
  • 无袖弓腰浑忘却 - Without sleeves, bowing waist, completely forgot.
  • 蛾眉空带九秋霜 - Beautiful eyebrows, in vain wearing the frost of nine autumns.
  • 如何是弓腰 - How is the bowing waist?
  • 乃反首髻及地 - Then turned the head backward, hair bun touching the ground.
  • 腰势如规 - Waist posture like a circle.
  • 士人惊惧，因叱之 - Scholar frightened, then scolded them.
  • 忽然上屏 - Suddenly disappeared from the screen.
  • 亦无其它 - No other.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 大历中 - The Dali period of the Tang Dynasty.
  • 元和初 - The early years of the Yuanhe era of the Tang Dynasty.
  • 长安 - The ancient name for Xi'an, the capital of the Tang Dynasty.
  • 踏春阳 - A traditional activity where people step on the spring sunshine, symbolizing the arrival of spring.
  • 无袖弓腰 - A dance move where the waist is bent backward, often seen in traditional Chinese dance.
  • 蛾眉 - Refers to beautiful eyebrows, often used to describe a woman's beauty.
  • 九秋霜 - A metaphor for the cold and harshness of autumn, often used to describe a woman's beauty that remains unchanged despite the passage of time.

Proper Nouns

  • 大历 - The Dali period of the Tang Dynasty.
  • 元和 - The Yuanhe era of the Tang Dynasty.
  • 长安 - The ancient name for Xi'an, the capital of the Tang Dynasty.
  • 庐州 - An ancient name for Luzhou, present-day Hefei, Anhui Province.

[207]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 鄭相在梁州 - Refers to Zheng Xiang, a historical figure, and Liangzhou, a region in ancient China.
  • 善總持敕勒之術 - Likely refers to Buddhist or Daoist techniques, but the exact meanings are unclear.
  • 日有數十人候門 - Suggests a high level of respect or demand for the monk's services.
  • 腊高稍倦 - Describes the monk's physical condition, indicating age and fatigue.
  • 鄭公頗敬之 - Zheng Gong's high respect for the monk indicates his status and influence.
  • 因求住城東隙地 - The monk's request to live in a vacant area suggests a desire for seclusion.
  • 鄭公為起草屋種植 - Zheng Gong's actions show his support and provision for the monk.
  • 有沙彌、行者各一人 - Terms for Buddhist disciples, indicating the monk's monastic status.
  • 暇日，智圓向陽科腳甲 - A mundane activity that provides insight into the monk's daily life.
  • 有婦人布衣，甚端麗 - Describes a woman's appearance, suggesting her social status or personal qualities.
  • 至階作禮 - A formal greeting that indicates respect or formality.
  • 智圓遽整衣，怪問 - The monk's reaction shows surprise or concern.
  • 弟子何由至此 - A common inquiry in classical Chinese texts, indicating a request for explanation.
  • 妾不幸夫亡而子幼小，老母危病 - Describes a woman's tragic situation, indicating her need for help.
  • 知和尚神咒助力，乞加救護 - Belief in the monk's divine powers, indicating his spiritual authority.
  • 貧道本厭城隍喧啾，兼煩於招謝 - The monk's statement about disliking the noise of the city and being troubled by constant requests.
  • 弟子母病，可就此為加持也 - The woman's request for the monk's spiritual assistance for her mother's illness.
  • 復再三泣請 - The woman's repeated weeping and begging for help.
  • 且言母病劇，不可舉扶 - The woman's description of her mother's severe illness and inability to be moved.
  • 亦哀而許之 - The monk's compassionate agreement to help.
  • 乃言從此向北二十余里一村，村側近有魯家莊，但訪韋十娘所居也 - The monk's directions to a specific village and location.
  • 詰朝如言行二十余里 - The monk's actions the next morning, going as he had said.
  • 歷訪悉無而返 - The monk's return without finding anything after visiting everywhere.
  • 來日婦人復至 - The woman's return the next day.
  • 僧責曰 - The monk's scolding of the woman.
  • 何差謬如此 - The monk's question about the woman's mistake.
  • 只去和尚所止處二三里耳 - The monk's statement about the distance to his residence.
  • 和尚慈悲，必為再往 - The woman's plea for the monk's compassion and another attempt.
  • 老僧衰暮，今誓不出 - The monk's determination not to go out due to his age and fatigue.
  • 婦人乃聲高曰 - The woman's raised voice and statement.
  • 慈悲何在耶 - The woman's rhetorical question about the monk's compassion.
  • 今事須去 - The woman's statement about the urgency of the matter.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 鄭相在梁州 - Refers to Zheng Xiang, a historical figure, and Liangzhou, a region in ancient China.
  • 善總持敕勒之術 - Likely refers to Buddhist or Daoist techniques, but the exact meanings are unclear.
  • 日有數十人候門 - Suggests a high level of respect or demand for the monk's services.
  • 腊高稍倦 - Describes the monk's physical condition, indicating age and fatigue.

  • 因求住城東隙地 - The monk's request to live in a vacant area suggests a desire for seclusion.
  • 鄭公為起草屋種植 - Zheng Gong's actions show his support and provision for the monk.
  • 有沙彌、行者各一人 - Terms for Buddhist disciples, indicating the monk's monastic status.

  • 智圓遽整衣，怪問 - The monk's reaction shows surprise or concern.
  • 弟子何由至此 - A common inquiry in classical Chinese texts, indicating a request for explanation.
  • 妾不幸夫亡而子幼小，老母危病 - Describes a woman's tragic situation, indicating her need for help.
  • 知和尚神咒助力，乞加救護 - Belief in the monk's divine powers, indicating his spiritual authority.

  • 復再三泣請 - The woman's repeated weeping and begging for help.
  • 且言母病劇，不可舉扶 - The woman's description of her mother's severe illness and inability to be moved.
  • 亦哀而許之 - The monk's compassionate agreement to help.
  • 乃言從此向北二十余里一村，村側近有魯家莊，但訪韋十娘所居也 - The monk's directions to a specific village and location.
  • 詰朝如言行二十余里 - The monk's actions the next morning, going as he had said.
  • 歷訪悉無而返 - The monk's return without finding anything after visiting everywhere.
  • 來日婦人復至 - The woman's return the next day.
  • 僧責曰 - The monk's scolding of the woman.
  • 何差謬如此 - The monk's question about the woman's mistake.
  • 只去和尚所止處二三里耳 - The monk's statement about the distance to his residence.
  • 和尚慈悲，必為再往 - The woman's plea for the monk's compassion and another attempt.
  • 老僧衰暮，今誓不出 - The monk's determination not to go out due to his age and fatigue.
  • 婦人乃聲高曰 - The woman's raised voice and statement.
  • 慈悲何在耶 - The woman's rhetorical question about the monk's compassion.
  • 今事須去 - The woman's statement about the urgency of the matter.

[208]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 因上阶牵僧臂 - Literal description of someone climbing the stairs and grabbing the monk's arm.
  • 惊迫，亦疑其非人 - Phrase indicating fear and suspicion, suggesting doubt about the identity of the person.
  • 恍惚间以刀子刺之 - Phrase describing a moment of confusion where someone stabs another with a knife.
  • 妇人遂倒 - Simple statement indicating that the woman fell down.
  • 沙弥误中刀，流血死矣 - Phrase indicating that the novice monk accidentally got stabbed and died from the wound.
  • 僧忙然 - Phrase indicating the monk's state of panic or confusion.
  • 遽与行者瘗之于饭瓮下 - Phrase describing the hurried burial of the body under a food container.
  • 盖魅所为也 - Phrase suggesting that the event was caused by a spirit or ghost.
  • 沙弥父母尽皆号哭诣僧 - Phrase describing the parents of the novice monk crying and going to see the monk.
  • 僧犹绐焉 - Phrase indicating that the monk still tried to deceive them.
  • 其父乃锹索而获 - Phrase describing the father digging up the body with a shovel.
  • 即诉于官 - Phrase indicating that the father immediately reported the incident to the authorities.
  • 郑公大骇 - Phrase indicating that the official was greatly shocked.
  • 俾求盗吏细按 - Phrase indicating that the official ordered a thief-hunting official to investigate thoroughly.
  • 意其必冤也 - Phrase indicating the official's belief that there must be some injustice involved.
  • 僧具陈状 - Phrase indicating that the monk presented his case or statement.
  • 贫道宿债，有死而已 - Phrase indicating the monk's belief that he had a debt to pay and that death was the only outcome.
  • 按者亦以死论 - Phrase indicating that the investigator also concluded that the monk should be punished with death.
  • 僧求假七日 - Phrase indicating that the monk requested seven days off.
  • 令持念为将来资粮 - Phrase indicating that the monk asked for prayers to be said for his future well-being.
  • 郑公哀而许之 - Phrase indicating that the official, moved by compassion, granted the request.
  • 僧沐浴设坛 - Phrase indicating that the monk bathed and set up an altar.
  • 凡三夕 - Phrase indicating that this process took place over three nights.
  • 妇人见于坛上 - Phrase indicating that the woman appeared on the altar.
  • 我类不少 - Phrase indicating that there are many of her kind.
  • 所求食处辄为和尚破除 - Phrase indicating that the places where they sought food were always destroyed by the monk.
  • 沙弥且在，能为誓不持念，必相还也 - Phrase indicating that if the novice monk is still alive, they will return if the monk swears not to hold any more prayers.
  • 智圆恳为设誓 - Phrase indicating that the monk sincerely made a vow.
  • 妇人喜曰 - Phrase indicating that the woman was pleased and spoke.
  • 沙弥在城南某村几里古丘中 - Phrase indicating that the novice monk was found in an ancient tomb in a village to the south of the city.
  • 神已痴矣 - Phrase indicating that the spirit was already confused or insane.
  • 发沙弥棺，中乃苕帚也 - Phrase indicating that when the coffin of the novice monk was opened, it contained only a broom.
  • 僧始得雪 - Phrase indicating that the monk was finally cleared of suspicion.
  • 自是绝珠贯，不复道一梵字 - Phrase indicating that from then on, the monk completely gave up his religious practices and no longer spoke any Sanskrit words.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 僧 - Refers to a Buddhist monk.
  • 沙弥 - Refers to a novice monk in the Buddhist tradition.
  • 行者 - Refers to a layperson who assists the monk.
  • 魅 - Refers to a spirit or ghost, often associated with supernatural phenomena.
  • 珠贯 - Refers to beads, often used in Buddhist practices.
  • 梵字 - Refers to Sanskrit script, commonly used in Buddhist texts and rituals.

Proper Nouns

  • 郑公 - Refers to a government official, likely a high-ranking figure.
  • 智圆 - The name of the monk involved in the story.
  • 沙弥 - The name of the novice monk.
  • 城南某村几里古丘 - Refers to a specific location where the novice monk was found.

[209]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 将为薪以求利 - To cut it into firewood to make a profit; indicates the intention to sell the wood.
  • 身如{尔虫}矣 - Transformed into a bug-like creature; describes a sudden, dramatic change.
  • 剪方寸贴之，血立止也 - Cut a small piece and apply it, the blood stops immediately; describes a traditional method of stopping bleeding.
  • 因是获利而归 - Gained wealth from this and returned home; summarizes the outcome of the event.
  • 遂钱成龙形 - Then cast the money into the shape of a dragon; describes the transformation of the money.
  • 裹粮钻火 - To pack food and start a fire; indicates preparation for a journey.
  • 境无遗跬 - Not leaving a single step unexplored; suggests thorough exploration.
  • 倒崖有光镜 - Cave or cliff face with a reflective surface; possibly a natural formation.
  • 大如钴钅莽 - As large as a drill bit; describes the size of the spider.
  • 长叫一声 - Let out a loud cry; describes a sudden, loud sound.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 元和 - Era name of Emperor Xianzong of Tang; indicates the historical period.
  • 洛阳 - Capital city of the Tang Dynasty; known for its cultural and economic significance.
  • 苏湛 - Historical or fictional traveler; mentioned in the text.
  • 蓬鹊山 - Mountain or region; possibly a fictional or less well-known location.
  • 裴 - Historical or fictional traveler or hunter; mentioned in the text.
  • 山蜘蛛 - Large spider; often associated with folklore and legends.
  • 车轮 - Metaphor for size; indicates the spider was very large.
  • 金创 - Wounds caused by metal weapons; a common injury in ancient times.

Proper Nouns

  • 王清 - Historical or fictional figure mentioned in the text.
  • 车轮 - Metaphor for size; indicates the spider was very large.
  • 金创 - Wounds caused by metal weapons; a common injury in ancient times.

[210]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 罢官 - Means 'to be dismissed from office' or 'to retire from public service.'
  • 食兼数人 - Means 'to eat as much as several people,' indicating a voracious appetite.
  • 尤能食 - '尤能' means 'especially able to,' and '食' refers to eating certain types of food, possibly fish or meat.
  • 常言味未尝果腹 - '常言' means 'often say,' and '未尝果腹' means 'never felt full,' suggesting dissatisfaction with previous meals.
  • 邑客乃网鱼百余斤 - '邑客' means 'guests from the town,' and '网鱼' means 'catching fish,' indicating a large quantity of fish caught by the guests.
  • 会于野亭 - '会' means 'to gather,' and '野亭' refers to a pavilion in the countryside.
  • 观其下箸 - '观' means 'to watch,' and '下箸' means 'to start eating,' indicating the act of beginning to eat.
  • 初食数叠 - '初' means 'at first,' '食' means 'to eat,' and '数叠' means 'several layers,' suggesting that the person ate multiple layers of food.
  • 忽似哽 - '忽' means 'suddenly,' and '似哽' means 'as if choking,' indicating a sudden choking sensation.
  • 咯出一骨珠子 - '咯出' means 'spit out,' and '骨珠子' refers to a bone bead, likely a small, round bone.
  • 大如黑豆 - '大如' means 'as large as,' and '黑豆' refers to black beans, indicating the size of the bone bead.

  • 夫人病，唯我能理，何不祈我 - Means 'your wife is ill, only I can treat her, why don't you ask me,' asserting the old woman's unique ability.
  • 刘素刚，咄之，姥徐戟手曰 - Means 'Liu, being usually stern, rebuked her, the old woman slowly pointed at him and said,' showing Liu's initial reaction and the old woman's calm response.
  • 妻因暴心痛，殆将卒，刘不得已祝之 - Means 'his wife then suffered sudden heart pain, almost dying, Liu had no choice but to pray to her,' describing the severity of her condition and his desperate action.
  • 言已复出，刘揖之坐，乃索茶一瓯 - Means 'after speaking, she reappeared, Liu bowed to her and invited her to sit, then asked for a cup of tea,' showing the old woman's return and Liu's respectful response.
  • 后时时辄出，家人亦不之惧 - Means 'later, she often appeared, the family no longer feared her,' indicating the recurring presence of the old woman and the family's growing acceptance.
  • 经年，复谓刘曰 - Means 'after a year, she again told Liu,' marking the passage of time and the old woman's return.
  • 我有女子及笄，烦主人求一佳婿 - Means 'I have a daughter who has come of age, please find her a good husband,' introducing a new request.
  • 人鬼路殊，固难遂所托 - Means 'the paths of humans and ghosts are different, it is indeed difficult to fulfill your request,' Liu's response.
  • 非求人也，但为刻桐木为形，稍上者则为佳矣 - Means 'it is not to find a human, but to carve a figure from paulownia wood, the better the carving, the better,' the old woman's clarification.
  • 刘许诺，因为具之 - Means 'Liu agreed and carved the figure for her,' showing his compliance.
  • 经宿，木人失矣 - Means 'after a night, the wooden figure was gone,' indicating the completion of the task.
  • 兼烦主人作铺公、铺母 - Means 'also ask the master to make a male and female figure,' another request.
  • 若可，某夕我自具车轮奉迎 - Means 'if possible, on a certain night I will bring a carriage to welcome them,' the old woman's promise.
  • 刘心计无奈何，亦许 - Means 'Liu, unable to refuse, also agreed,' showing his resignation.
  • 至一日过酉，有仆马车乘至门 - Means 'on a day past the hour of the rooster, a carriage with servants and horses arrived at the door,' marking the arrival.
  • 刘与妻各登其车马 - Means 'Liu and his wife each got into their respective carriages,' showing their departure.

  • 宾客供帐之盛，如王公家 - Means 'the guests and furnishings were as splendid as those of a prince or noble,' describing the opulence.
  • 引刘至一厅，朱紫数十 - Means 'they led Liu to a hall where there were dozens of officials in red and purple robes,' showing the high status of the attendees.

  • 但相视而已 - Means 'they merely look at each other,' suggesting a sense of mutual recognition or acknowledgment without further interaction.
  • 及五更，刘与妻恍惚间却还至家 - '及五更' means 'by the fifth watch of the night' (around 3-5 AM). '恍惚间' means 'in a daze' or 'in a trance-like state.' '却还至家' means 'return home unexpectedly.'
  • 十不记其一二矣 - Means 'he could not remember even a tenth of it,' indicating that the experience was so vivid and overwhelming that it is hard to recall.
  • 姥随枕而灭 - Means 'the old woman disappeared with the pillow,' suggesting a magical or supernatural event.
  • 妻遂疾发，刘与男女酹地祷之，不复出矣 - '遂疾发' means 'she suddenly fell ill.' '酹地祷之' means 'pour wine on the ground and pray.' '不复出矣' means 'she never came out again.'
  • 妻竟以心痛卒 - Means 'his wife eventually died of heartache.'
  • 刘妹复病心痛，刘欲徙居 - '刘妹' means 'Liu's sister.' '复病心痛' means 'fell ill again with heartache.' '刘欲徙居' means 'Liu wanted to move.'
  • 一切物胶着其处，轻若履屣亦不可举 - '一切物胶着其处' means 'everything was stuck in place.' '轻若履屣亦不可举' means 'even if it were as light as a shoe, it could not be moved.'
  • 迎道流上章，梵僧持咒，悉不禁 - '迎道流上章' means 'invoked Taoist priests to write charms.' '梵僧持咒' means 'invoked Buddhist monks to recite mantras.' '悉不禁' means 'none of it worked.'
  • 刘尝暇日读药方，其婢小碧自外来，垂手缓步，大言 - '刘尝暇日读药方' means 'Liu read a medical prescription during his leisure time.' '其婢小碧自外来' means 'his maid Xiao Bi came from outside.' '垂手缓步' means 'with her hands hanging and walking slowly.' '大言' means 'spoke loudly.'
  • 刘四颇忆平昔无 - Means 'Liu Si (a nickname for Liu) remembered nothing from the past.'
  • 省近从泰山回，路逢飞天野叉携贤妹心肝，我亦夺得 - '省近从泰山回' means 'recently returned from Mount Tai.' '路逢飞天野叉携贤妹心肝' means 'encountered a flying demon carrying my sister's heart and liver.' '我亦夺得' means 'I also took them.'
  • 又觉袖中风生，冲帘幌，入堂中 - '又觉袖中风生' means 'she felt wind coming from her sleeve.' '冲帘幌' means 'blowing through the curtains.' '入堂中' means 'entering the hall.'
  • 乃上堂对刘坐，问存殁，叙平生事 - '乃上堂对刘坐' means 'she then went upstairs and sat facing Liu.' '问存殁' means 'asked about the living and the dead.' '叙平生事' means 'recited her life story.'
  • 刘与杜省躬同年及第，有分，其婢举止笑语无不肖也 - '刘与杜省躬同年及第' means 'Liu and Du Xiangong were the same year to pass the imperial examinations.' '有分' means 'had a connection.' '其婢举止笑语无不肖也' means 'her maid's mannerisms and speech were all similar.'
  • 婢忽然而倒，及觉，一无所记 - '婢忽然而倒' means 'the maid suddenly fainted.' '及觉，一无所记' means 'when she woke up, she remembered nothing.'
  • 其妹亦自此无恙 - Means 'his sister also recovered from her illness.'

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 劉元鼎為蔡州蔡州新破 - Ambiguous phrase; could mean 'Liu Yuanding was in charge of Cai Prefecture, which had recently been conquered' or 'Liu Yuanding was in charge of Cai Prefecture, which had recently been captured.'
  • 食（一曰仓）场狐暴 - ‘食’ likely means 'grain storage' or 'granary,' and ‘场狐暴’ refers to foxes causing trouble in the grain storage area. Note ‘(一曰仓)’ suggests an alternative reading of ‘仓’ for ‘食.’
  • 日于球场纵犬逐之为乐 - ‘球场’ likely refers to a large open space or courtyard, not necessarily a modern football field. ‘纵犬逐之’ means 'to let dogs chase them,' and ‘为乐’ means 'for amusement.'
  • 经年，所杀百数 - ‘经年’ means 'over the course of a year,' and ‘所杀百数’ means 'hundreds were killed.'
  • 后获一疥狐 - ‘疥狐’ refers to a sick or diseased fox.
  • 纵五六犬皆不敢逐，狐亦不走 - ‘纵五六犬’ means 'let out five or six dogs,' and ‘皆不敢逐’ means 'none dared to chase.' ‘狐亦不走’ means 'the fox did not run away.'
  • 刘大异之 - ‘大异之’ means 'found it very strange.'
  • 令访大将家猎狗及监军亦自夸巨犬 - ‘令访’ means 'ordered to visit,' ‘大将家猎狗’ means 'the general's hunting dogs,' and ‘监军亦自夸巨犬’ means 'the military inspector also boasted of his large dogs.'
  • 至皆弭耳环守之 - ‘弭耳’ means 'lowered ears,' and ‘环守之’ means 'stood around it.'
  • 狐良久才跳，直上设厅，穿台盘出厅后，及城墙，俄失所在 - ‘狐良久才跳’ means 'the fox jumped after a long time,' ‘直上设厅’ means 'straight up to the hall,' ‘穿台盘出厅后’ means 'through the platform and out behind the hall,' ‘及城墙’ means 'to the city wall,' and ‘俄失所在’ means 'soon disappeared.'
  • 刘自是不复令捕 - ‘自是’ means 'from then on,' and ‘不复令捕’ means 'no longer ordered to catch.'
  • 道术中有天狐别行法 - ‘道术’ refers to Daoist practices, ‘天狐’ means 'heavenly fox,' and ‘别行法’ means 'special method.'
  • 言天狐九尾金色，役于日月宫，有符有醮日 - ‘言’ means 'says,' ‘九尾金色’ means 'nine-tailed golden,' ‘役于日月宫’ means 'serves in the palace of the sun and moon,' and ‘有符有醮日’ means 'has talismans and ritual days.'
  • 可洞达阴阳 - ‘可洞达’ means 'can penetrate,' and ‘阴阳’ refers to yin and yang, the dual forces in Chinese cosmology.
  • 南中有兽名风狸 - ‘南中’ refers to the southern regions, and ‘风狸’ is a type of animal.
  • 如狙，眉长好羞，见人辄低头 - ‘如狙’ means 'like a monkey,' ‘眉长好羞’ means 'with long eyebrows and a shy demeanor,' and ‘见人辄低头’ means 'lowers its head when it sees people.'
  • 其溺能理风疾 - ‘其溺’ means 'its urine,' and ‘理风疾’ means 'cures wind-related illnesses.'
  • 卫士多言风狸杖难得于翳形草 - ‘卫士’ means 'guards,' ‘多言’ means 'often say,' ‘风狸杖’ means 'wind-lizard staff,' and ‘难得于翳形草’ means 'hard to find in the magic herb.'
  • 南人以上长绳系于野外大树下，入匿于旁树穴中伺之 - ‘南人’ means 'southern people,' ‘以上长绳’ means 'using a long rope,' ‘系于野外大树下’ means 'tied to a big tree in the wild,' ‘入匿于旁树穴中’ means 'hid in a hole near another tree,' and ‘伺之’ means 'waited for it.'
  • 三日后，知无人至，乃于草中寻摸 - ‘三日后’ means 'three days later,' ‘知无人至’ means 'knew no one came,' and ‘乃于草中寻摸’ means 'then searched through the grass.'
  • 忽得一草茎，折之长尺许，窥树上有鸟集，指之，随指而堕，因取而食之 - ‘忽得一草茎’ means 'suddenly found a stem of grass,' ‘折之长尺许’ means 'broke it off about a foot long,' ‘窥树上有鸟集’ means 'saw a bird perched on the tree,' ‘指之’ means 'pointed at it,' ‘随指而堕’ means 'it fell when pointed at,' and ‘因取而食之’ means 'then took it and ate it.'
  • 人候其怠，劲走夺之 - ‘人候其怠’ means 'people waited for it to be tired,' and ‘劲走夺之’ means 'ran quickly to take it.'
  • 见人遽啮食之，或不及，则弃于草中 - ‘见人遽啮食之’ means 'saw people and bit them to eat,' ‘或不及’ means 'sometimes not reaching,' and ‘则弃于草中’ means 'then threw it into the grass.'
  • 若不可下，当打之数百，方肯为人取 - ‘若不可下’ means 'if it doesn't come down,' ‘当打之数百’ means 'should hit it hundreds of times,' and ‘方肯为人取’ means 'only then will it be taken by humans.'
  • 有得之者，禽兽随指而毙 - ‘有得之者’ means 'those who got it,' and ‘禽兽随指而毙’ means 'birds and animals die when pointed at.'
  • 有所欲者，指之如意 - ‘有所欲者’ means 'those who want something,' and ‘指之如意’ means 'point at it and get what they want.'

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 处士 - A term for a scholar or literatus who does not hold an official position; a title of respect for someone with learning but not in government service.
  • 厕于荒冢上 - To sit on/defecate on a desolate grave; the verb '厕' here is used in the sense of 'to sit' or 'to defecate' (a homonymic use), which is archaic and may be ambiguous in translation.
  • 偶有良药 - By chance, there is a good medicine; the phrase is idiomatic and suggests the elder has a remedy that is not commonly available.
  • 下日不开必愈 - If you do not open it the next day, it will surely heal; '下日' is likely 'the next day' or 'the day after.'
  • 至死不差 - Until death, it did not improve; '差' here means to improve or recover.
  • 小者如榆钱，大者如钱 - Small ones like榆钱 (a type of nut or seed from the榆 tree), large ones like coins; this is a simile to describe the size of the sores.

  • 荆秀才 - A scholar from Jingzhou; '秀才' is a traditional Chinese educational title, a low-level degree in the imperial examination system.
  • 杜晔 - A personal name, likely a historical figure or a fictional character; no widely known historical figure by this name is immediately identifiable, so it should be left as is unless further research identifies it.

  • 侯又玄 - A personal name; should be transliterated as 'Hou Youxuan.'
  • 杜晔 - Another personal name; transliterated as 'Du Ye.'
  • 秀才 - A title; should be translated as 'a scholar' or 'a xiucai' (with an explanation if needed in context).

  • 木再花，夏有雹 - This phrase must be read as two separate statements indicating that if trees bloom again, there will be hail in summer.
  • 李再花，秋大霜 - This phrase must be read as two separate statements indicating that if plums bloom again, there will be heavy frost in autumn.
  • 木无故丛生，枝尽向下，又生及一尺至一丈自死，皆凶 - This phrase must be read as a single statement indicating that if trees grow in clusters without reason, with branches all pointing downwards, and grow up to one foot to one yard and then die, it is all ominous.
  • 邑中终岁无鸟，有寇 - This phrase must be read as a single statement indicating that if there are no birds in the town all year round, there will be bandits.
  • 郡中忽无鸟者，日乌亡 - This phrase must be read as a single statement indicating that if there are suddenly no birds in the county, the sun and birds will disappear.
  • 鸡无故自飞去，家有蛊 - This phrase must be read as a single statement indicating that if chickens fly away without reason, there is a pest in the house.
  • 鸡日中不下树，妻妾奸谋 - This phrase must be read as a single statement indicating that if chickens do not come down from trees at noon, wives and concubines plot.
  • 见蛇交，三年死 - This phrase must be read as a single statement indicating that if one sees snakes mating, they will die within three years.
  • 蛇冬见寝室，主兵急 - This phrase must be read as a single statement indicating that if snakes appear in the bedroom in winter, there will be urgent military matters.
  • 人夜卧无故失髻者，鼠妖也 - This phrase must be read as a single statement indicating that if a person loses their hairpin while sleeping at night without reason, it is a mouse omen.
  • 屋柱木无故生芝者，白为丧，赤为血，黑为贼，黄为喜 - This phrase must be read as a single statement indicating that if mushrooms grow on the wooden pillars of the house without reason, white ones indicate mourning, red ones indicate bloodshed, black ones indicate thieves, and yellow ones indicate joy.
  • 其形如人面者亡财，如牛马者远役，如龟蛇者田蚕耗 - This phrase must be read as a single statement indicating that if something is shaped like a human face, it indicates losing money; if shaped like a cow or horse, it indicates long-distance labor; if shaped like a turtle or snake, it indicates damage to fields and silkworms.
  • 德及幽隐，则比目鱼至（一曰生） - This phrase must be read as a single statement indicating that if virtue reaches the hidden, paired fish arrive (one says they are born).
  • 妾媵有制，则白燕来巢 - This phrase must be read as a single statement indicating that if concubines are regulated, white swallows build nests.
  • 山上有葱，下有银 - This phrase must be read as a single statement indicating that if there are onions on the mountain, silver is found below.
  • 山上有薤，下有金 - This phrase must be read as a single statement indicating that if there are leeks on the mountain, gold is found below.
  • 山上有姜，下有铜锡 - This phrase must be read as a single statement indicating that if there are ginger on the mountain, copper and tin are found below.
  • 山有宝玉，木旁枝皆下垂 - This phrase must be read as a single statement indicating that if there are precious stones on the mountain, branches of trees hang down on the sides.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 雄雌夕旦鸣各异 - Means 'the male and female phoenixes have different calls in the evening and morning,' indicating a behavioral characteristic.
  • 黄帝使伶伦制十二写之 - Refers to the Yellow Emperor (黄帝) commissioning Ling Lun to create the twelve notes of music based on the phoenix's calls.
  • 其雄声，其雌音 - Describes the distinct sounds of the male and female phoenixes.
  • 乐有凤凰台 - Refers to the Phoenix Terrace, a place associated with music and the phoenix.
  • 此凤脚，下物如白石者 - Describes the phoenix's foot and a stone-like object beneath it.
  • 凤有时来仪，候其所止处，掘深三尺，有圆石如卵，正白，服之安心神 - Describes the phoenix's occasional appearance and the discovery of a white stone when it lands.
  • 因雷声而孕 - Means 'conceive due to the sound of thunder,' referring to the breeding of peacocks.
  • 群鹳旋飞为鹳井 - Describes a phenomenon where flocks of cranes fly in circles, creating a 'crane well.'
  • 鹤亦好旋飞，必有风雨 - Indicates that cranes also fly in circles, often preceding rain or wind.
  • 人探巢取鹳子，六十里早 - Means that if someone enters a crane's nest to take its eggs, the cranes will return early from a distance of sixty miles.
  • 能群飞薄霄激雨，雨为之散 - Describes the cranes' ability to fly in groups, touch the sky, and cause rain to disperse.
  • 乌鸣地上无好声 - Means 'crows cawing on the ground make no pleasant sound.'
  • 人临行，乌鸣而前引，多喜 - Describes a situation where crows caw in front of a person, which is considered a sign of good fortune.
  • 俗候乌飞翅重，天将雨 - Indicates that people observe the weight of crows' wings to predict rain.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 燕见之则毛脱 - Belief that swallows seeing certain animals will cause their feathers to fall out.
  • 燕蛰于水（一曰月）底 - Folk belief that swallows hibernate at the bottom of water or the moon.
  • 取桐为男女各一，投井中，燕必来 - Folk ritual involving placing two pieces of paulownia wood in a well to attract swallows.
  • 胸班黑，声大，名胡燕 - Description of a swallow with black chest markings and a loud voice, known as a 'Hu swallow.'

  • 蜀吊乌山，至雉雀来吊最悲 - Story about a mountain in Shu where pheasants and sparrows come to mourn, showing the deepest sorrow.

  • 鸽能飞行数千里，辄放一只至家，以为平安信 - Belief that pigeons can fly thousands of miles and are used to send messages of safety.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 鹦鹉，能飞 - Literal: 'Parrot, can fly.' Emphasizes the parrot's ability.
  • 众鸟趾前三后一，唯鹦鹉四趾齐分 - Literal: 'All birds have three toes in front and one behind, except for the parrot, which has four toes evenly distributed.' Highlights the unique anatomy of the parrot.
  • 凡鸟下睑眨上，独此鸟两睑俱动，如人目 - Literal: 'All birds blink their lower eyelids, but this bird (the parrot) moves both eyelids, like a human's eyes.' Emphasizes the parrot's unique eye movement.
  • 玄宗时，有五色鹦鹉能言，上令左右试牵帝衣，鸟辄目叱咤 - Literal: 'During the reign of Emperor Xuanzong, there was a five-colored parrot that could speak. The emperor ordered his attendants to try pulling his robe, and the bird would glare and scold.' Describes a specific incident involving the parrot.
  • 岐府文学能延京献《鹦鹉篇》，以赞其事 - Literal: 'The literary official of Qi Prefecture, Neng Yanjing, presented the 'Parrot Ode' to praise this event.' Indicates a literary work dedicated to the parrot.
  • 张燕公有表贺，称为时乐鸟 - Literal: 'Zhang Yan, the Duke of Yan, submitted a memorial to congratulate and called it the 'joyful bird of the time.' Shows the parrot's significance and the official recognition it received.
  • 杜鹃，始阳相催而鸣，先鸣者吐血死 - Literal: 'The cuckoo bird, at the beginning of spring, calls to each other. The first to call dies of blood.' Describes a mythological or symbolic aspect of the cuckoo.
  • 雏鹆，旧言可使取火，效人言，胜鹦鹉 - Literal: 'The young magpie, according to old sayings, can be made to fetch fire, mimic human speech, and surpass the parrot.' Highlights the magpie's abilities.
  • 取其目睛，和人乳研，滴眼中，能见烟霄外物也 - Literal: 'Take its eyeball, mix it with human milk, grind it, and drop it into the eye, and one can see things in the smoke and sky.' Describes a magical or alchemical process.
  • 鹅，济南郡张公城西北有鹅浦 - Literal: 'In the northwest of Zhanggong City in Jizhou Prefecture, there is a goose pond.' Provides a geographical context.
  • 南燕世，有渔人居水侧，常听鹅之声。众中有铃声甚清亮，候之，见一鹅咽颈极长，罗得之，项上有铜铃，缀以银锁，隐起“元鼎元年”字 - Literal: 'During the Southern Yan period, there was a fisherman living by the water. He often heard the sound of geese. Among the sounds, there was a very clear and bright ringing. He waited for it and saw a goose with an extremely long neck. He caught it and found a copper bell on its neck, attached with a silver lock, with the characters 'Yuan Ding Yuan Year' raised.' Describes a specific incident involving a goose.
  • 晋时，营道县令何潜之，于县界得鸟，大如白鹭，膝上髀下自然有铜贯之 - Literal: 'During the Jin dynasty, the magistrate of Yingdao County, He Qianzhi, found a bird in the county that was as large as a white heron, with natural copper rings on its thighs.' Describes a specific incident involving a bird.
  • ，旧言辟火灾 - Literal: 'The phoenix, according to old sayings, can ward off fire.' Describes a mythological attribute of the phoenix.
  • 巢于高树，生子穴中，衔其母翅飞下养之 - Literal: 'It nests in tall trees, lays eggs in holes, and carries its mother's wings down to feed them.' Describes the nesting and feeding behavior of the phoenix.

  • 犀之通天者必恶影，常饮浊水 - Means 'Rhinos with horns that reach the sky must hate their shadows and always drink murky water,' where '通天' describes a particularly tall or powerful horn.

  • 先于山路多植木，如狙┉ - Means 'They plant many trees along mountain paths, like monkeys,' where '如狙┉' is a metaphorical description.
  • 云犀前脚直，常倚木而息，木栏折则不能起 - Means 'They say that rhinos have straight front legs and often rest against trees; if the tree breaks, they cannot get up,' where '倚木而息' describes resting against a tree.
  • 犀牛一名奴角，有鸩处必有犀也 - Means 'Rhinos are also called 'slave horns'; where there are rhinos, there are also venomous snakes,' where '奴角' is a nickname and '有鸩处必有犀也' is a proverb.
  • 犀，三毛一孔 - Means 'Rhinos have three hairs and one hole,' where '三毛一孔' is a specific anatomical description.
  • 刘孝标言，犀堕角埋之，以假角易之 - Means 'Liu Xiaobiao said that if a rhino loses its horn, it buries it and replaces it with a fake one,' where '堕角' means losing the horn and '易之' means replacing it.

  • 遇大鱼辄放墨，方数尺，以混其身 - The sequence of actions (releasing ink, creating a cloud, obscuring the body) must be read as a single event.
  • 江东人或取墨书契，以脱人财物 - The sentence describes a folkloric practice of using the cuttlefish's ink to write contracts.
  • 海人言，昔秦皇东游，弃算袋于海，化为此鱼 - The sentence introduces a folkloric tale about the origin of the cuttlefish.
  • 形如算袋，两带极长 - The description of the cuttlefish's shape and tentacles must be read together.
  • 一说乌贼有碇，遇风则蚪前一须下碇 - The sentence introduces an alternative explanation for the cuttlefish's behavior.
  • 舀鱼，凡诸鱼欲产，舀鱼辄舐其腹 - The sentence describes the 舀鱼's role in helping other fish during childbirth.
  • 世谓之众鱼之生母 - The sentence concludes with a folkloric title for the 舀鱼.
  • 昔鱼，章安县出 - The sentence introduces the 昔鱼 and its origin.
  • 出入昔腹，子朝出索食，暮入母腹 - The sentence describes the 昔鱼's daily routine of leaving its mother's mouth in the morning to search for food and returning at night.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 异果 - General term for unusual or exotic fruits, often used in classical texts to describe rare or extraordinary fruits.
  • 忽失所在 - Literally 'suddenly lost its place,' meaning the sheep disappeared without a trace.
  • 形色鸣吼异常 - Describes the sheep's appearance, color, and sound as unusual or strange.
  • 遂独行 - Means 'then it went off alone,' describing the sheep's subsequent behavior.
  • 豁然明朗 - Literally 'suddenly bright and clear,' describing the sudden change in the environment.
  • 花木皆非人间所有 - Means 'the flowers and trees were all unlike anything in the world,' indicating a fantastical or otherworldly setting.
  • 切一将还 - Means 'cut one and took it back,' describing the shepherd's action with the fruit.
  • 为鬼所夺 - Means 'was taken by a ghost,' indicating the supernatural event.
  • 复往取此果 - Means 'went back to take the fruit again,' describing the shepherd's return.
  • 结窠于甘树 - Means 'built a nest on the sugar cane tree,' describing the behavior of the ants.
  • 甘实在其窠中 - Means 'the sugar cane was inside the nest,' indicating the ants' interaction with the sugar cane.
  • 冬深取之 - Means 'took it in the deep winter,' describing the timing of the action.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 赡披国 - A fictional or obscure country, possibly a literary creation or a reference to a distant, unknown land.
  • 天宝十年 - The 10th year of the Tianbao era (751 CE) during the Tang Dynasty, a period of prosperity and cultural flourishing.
  • 玄宗幸蜀年 - The year when Emperor Xuanzong of Tang fled to Sichuan during the An Lushan Rebellion (756 CE).

  • 研饮之，愈众疾，尤治邪气 - Indicates that grinding the peach into a drink can cure various diseases, especially those caused by evil spirits.

  • 随伐随长 - Means that the tree would grow back immediately after being cut down.

  • 不庇凡草，不止恶禽 - Means that the tree does not shelter common plants or harmful birds.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 构 - Refers to a type of plant or tree, possibly 'mulberry' (桑) or 'hemp' (麻), but the exact meaning is uncertain due to the archaic character .
  • 田久废，必生构 - Literally ' fields, long abandoned, must grow 构,' suggesting that 构 is a hardy plant that thrives in neglected land.
  • 叶有办曰楮，五曰构 - A classification of leaves: '办' (bàn) is a rare term, possibly referring to a type of leaf or plant, while '楮' (chǔ) is a specific plant (paper mulberry). '五曰构' suggests a fifth category, but the meaning is unclear.
  • 性难长 - Literally 'nature is hard to grow,' indicating that the plant is slow-growing or difficult to cultivate.
  • 世重黄杨以无火 - The world values 黄杨 because it does not burn, suggesting that 黄杨 is prized for its fire-resistant properties.
  • 以水试之 - Literally 'test it with water,' a method to determine if the wood is fire-resistant.
  • 沉则无火 - If it sinks, it does not burn, indicating that the wood is dense and fire-resistant.
  • 取此木必以阴晦 - Take this wood on overcast days, suggesting that the wood should be harvested under specific weather conditions.
  • 夜无一星则伐之 - Cut it down on nights without a single star, possibly indicating a superstitious belief about the best time to harvest.
  • 为枕不裂 - Used as a pillow, it does not crack, highlighting the wood's durability.
  • 蒲萄，俗言蒲萄蔓好引于西南 - Popular saying: The grapevine is best trained to the southwest, suggesting a traditional gardening practice.
  • 遂大得蒲萄 - I greatly obtained grapes, indicating a large harvest or abundance.
  • 奇有滋味 - Amazingly flavorful, praising the taste of the grapes.
  • 作何形状 - What shape does it have? A direct question about the appearance of the grapes.
  • 有类软枣 - It resembles soft dates, comparing the grapes to another fruit.
  • 殊不体物 - You do not understand the nature of things, criticizing the comparison.
  • 可得言似生荔枝 - It can be said to resemble fresh litchis, suggesting a more accurate comparison.
  • 魏武有言 - Cao Cao (Wei Wu) once said, referencing a historical figure.
  • 末夏涉秋，尚有余暑 - At the end of summer, entering autumn, there is still lingering heat, describing the climate.
  • 酒醉宿醒，掩露而食 - After a night of drinking, waking up in the morning, eating while shielding from the dew, describing a specific dining experience.
  • 甘而不饴，酸而不酢 - Sweet but not overly sugary, sour but not excessively acidic, praising the balanced flavor.
  • 道之固以流味称奇，况亲食之者 - Even describing it is enough to make its flavor seem extraordinary, let alone those who actually eat it, emphasizing the grapes' exceptional taste.
  • 此物实出于大宛 - This fruit truly comes from Daxia (a historical region), indicating its origin.
  • 张骞所致 - Brought by Zhang Qian, referencing the famous explorer who introduced grapes to China.
  • 有黄、白、黑三种 - There are yellow, white, and black varieties, describing the grape colors.
  • 成熟之时，子实逼侧，星编珠聚 - When mature, the clusters are tightly packed, like stars or pearls, describing the grape clusters.
  • 西域多酿以为酒 - In the Western Regions, it is often made into wine, indicating its use in winemaking.
  • 每来岁贡 - It is often presented as tribute, highlighting its status.
  • 在汉西京，似亦不少 - In the Western Capital of the Han Dynasty, it seems to be plentiful, indicating its availability.
  • 杜陵田五十亩，中有蒲萄百树 - In the fifty acres of land in Duling, there are a hundred grape trees, describing a specific example.
  • 今在京兆，非直止禁林也 - Now in Jingzhao, it is not just in the imperial gardens, suggesting its widespread cultivation.
  • 乃园种户植，接荫连架 - It is planted in gardens and households, indicating its widespread cultivation. Its shade extends and its vines connect, describing the grapevines' growth.
  • 其味何如橘柚 - How does its taste compare to oranges and pomelos? A direct question about the grapes' flavor.
  • 津液奇胜，芬芳减之 - Its juice is exceptionally sweet, praising the grapes' sweetness. Its fragrance is slightly less, indicating that the grapes are less fragrant than oranges and pomelos.
  • 金衣素裹，见苞作贡 - Golden skin, pure wrapping, describing the grapes' appearance. Presented in clusters as tribute, highlighting its status.
  • 向齿自消，良应不及 - It melts in the mouth, describing its texture. Its excellence should be beyond compare, praising the grapes' quality.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • 构，田久废，必生构。叶有办曰楮，五曰构。 - The sentence is elliptical and requires interpretation of the rare character  and the unclear classification of '办' and '楮.'
  • 或曰以水试之，沉则无火。 - The sentence is a methodological statement that must be read as a whole to understand the test for fire resistance.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 窠 - In the phrase '牡丹一窠', refers to a cluster or bush of peonies, not a 'nest.'
  • 着花 - Classical expression meaning 'to bear flowers' or 'to bloom.'
  • 正晕、倒晕 - Terms describing color gradations or shades in petals. '正晕' may mean 'central hue' or 'primary color,' while '倒晕' might be 'reversed gradient' or 'outer hue.'
  • 重台花 - Refers to 'double-flowered' or 'multi-petaled' peonies with a 'flower face' of 7–8 inches in diameter.
  • 无抹心者 - Technical botanical term. '抹心' may refer to the center of the flower or a central stigma, suggesting some peonies lack this feature.
  • 合欢 - A type of flower, but also a term for harmony or union. The flower '合欢' (Albizia julibrissin) is known for its bilateral symmetry and delicate, feathery petals.
  • 金灯 - A plant name. Also known as 'golden lamp' or 'Chinese lantern plant' (Physalis alkekengi), with bright orange or red calyces resembling lanterns.
  • 无义草 - A pejorative name for the plant, meaning 'grass of no virtue' or 'plant of no loyalty.' Likely a folk name implying the plant is unfit for cultivation in homes.
  • 合离 - A botanical term referring to a plant with a tuberous root (芋魁), and '游子十二环' may describe the root structure or growth pattern. It is a plant that grows in clusters but is not connected at the root.
  • 赤箭 - A botanical name for 'red arrow,' likely referring to Cyperus rotundus or another grass-like plant. Also a mythical herb in Chinese alchemy associated with immortality or spiritual power.

  • 元和 - A reign period of Emperor Xianzong of the Tang dynasty (806–820 CE).

  • 素师院 - May refer to a monastic courtyard or garden within the Xingshan Temple, associated with a monk or master named Sushī.
  • 合欢 - A specific plant name with distinct botanical characteristics and cultural meanings in Chinese tradition.

  • 七月二十日为上时 - Indicates that the first period for capturing eagles is from the 20th day of the seventh month.
  • 内地者多，塞外者殊少 - Suggests that eagles are more commonly found inland than in the border regions.

  • 螽虫好食网 - Indicates that grasshoppers tend to eat the net, so precautions are taken.

  • 出壳之后，即于窠外放巢 - Describes the behavior of birds after their chicks hatch, where they place the nest outside the shell.
  • 大鸷恐其坠堕及为日所曝，热致损 - Means 'large birds fear that their chicks will fall or be exposed to the sun, which can cause harm,' indicating the protective measures taken by parent birds.
  • 乃取带叶树枝插其巢畔，防其坠堕及作阴凉也 - Describes the method of placing leafy branches around the nest to prevent falls and provide shade.
  • 欲验雏之大小，以所插之叶为候 - Means 'to check the size of the chicks, use the leaves as a sign,' suggesting a method to monitor the growth of the chicks.
  • 若一日二日，其叶虽萎而尚带青色 - Describes the condition of the leaves after one or two days, where they are still slightly green.
  • 至六七日，其叶微黄 - Indicates that after six or seven days, the leaves turn slightly yellow.
  • 十日后枯瘁，此时雏渐大可取 - Means 'after ten days, the leaves wither, and at this point, the chicks are large enough to be taken,' suggesting the optimal time to capture the chicks.

  • The sentence describes the demon's reaction and exit, which can be read as a sequence of actions or a single event.
  • The sentence describes the monk's search and discovery, which can be read as a series of actions or a single event.
  • The sentence describes the monk's action and the result, which can be read as a cause-and-effect relationship.
  • The sentence describes the physical evidence of human intervention, which can be read as a description of the carving or a cause-and-effect relationship.
  • The sentence describes the final resolution, which can be read as a single action or a cause-and-effect relationship.

  • 忽变为巨星，腾起数道烛天 - Suddenly transformed into a giant star, rising several times to illuminate the sky; '巨星' (jùngxīng) means a giant star, and '烛天' (zhútiān) means to illuminate the sky.
  • 左右取烛索鹄于草积中，已卒矣 - The attendants lit candles to search for Li Hu among the hay, but he was already dead; '左右' (zuǒyòu) refers to the attendants or servants.
  • 半日方苏 - He only regained consciousness after half a day; '方苏' means to regain consciousness.
  • 因说所见 - Then recounted what he had seen; '因' means 'then' or 'thereupon'.
  • 未旬，无病而死 - Within ten days, he died without any illness; '未旬' means within ten days.
  • 常夜习业 - Often studied at night; '习业' means to study or practice one's profession.
  • 忽见一小鬼{髟曾}，头长二尺余，满头碎光如星 - Suddenly saw a small ghost with a head longer than two feet, covered with tiny lights like stars; '{髟曾}' (zéng) describes a hairstyle or appearance, possibly referring to a ghost or spirit.
  • 眨眨（一作荧荧）可恶 - Blinking (or flickering) and annoying; '眨眨' (zhāzhā) means blinking, and '荧荧' (yíngyíng) means flickering.
  • 戏灯弄砚，纷搏不止 - Playing with the lamp and inkstone, struggling incessantly; '纷搏' (fēnbó) means to struggle or fight.
  • 学生素有胆，叱之，稍却，复傍书案 - The student, who was usually brave, shouted at it, and it retreated slightly, then returned to the side of the desk; '素有胆' means 'usually brave'.
  • 因伺其所为，渐逼近，乙因擒之 - He then watched its actions, gradually approaching, and eventually captured it; '伺' (sì) means to watch or observe.
  • 踞坐求哀，辞颇苦切 - It sat down and begged for mercy, its words being very sad and urgent; '踞坐' (jùzuò) means to sit cross-legged, and '辞颇苦切' means the words were very sad and urgent.
  • 天将晓，觉如物折声，视之，乃弊木杓也，其上粘粟百余粒 - As dawn approached, he heard a sound like something breaking, and when he looked, it was an old wooden ladle with over a hundred grains of millet stuck to it; '天将晓' means 'as dawn approached,' and '弊木杓' (bì mù sháo) means an old wooden ladle.

  • 來春，明經與鄰房三人夢中所訪者，悉及第 - The following spring, the scholar and the three people he visited in his dream all passed the exam, confirming the dream's prediction.

  • 撤榻掘地 - Means 'to remove the bed and dig the ground,' symbolic of preparation or excavation.
  • 捧匣授之而卒 - Means 'to hold the box and give it to him, and then die,' symbolic of legacy or inheritance.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 病疮于首 - 指头部生疮。
  • 托后事于女婿 - 指将后事托付给女婿。
  • 予年二十九为校书郎 - 指韦温二十九岁时担任校书郎。
  • 梦涯水中流，见二吏赍牒相召 - 指韦温梦见在水中流，看见两个吏人拿着文书互相召唤。
  • 一吏至，言彼坟至大，功须万日，今未也 - 指一个吏人到，说那坟墓非常大，需要万日才能完成，现在还没有完成。
  • 不累日而卒 - 指韦温没有几天就去世了。
  • 乘凉于庭际 - 指崔汾仲在庭院中乘凉。
  • 疏旷月色 - 指稀疏的月色。
  • 觉有异香 - 指感到有奇异的香气。
  • 南垣土动簌簌 - 指南墙的土动簌簌。
  • 大言曰 - 指道士大声说。
  • 予岂逃乎 - 指韦温说他岂能逃脱。
  • 缓步庭中 - 指道士缓慢地在庭院中行走。
  • 年可四十 - 指道士年龄大约四十岁。

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 永寧王相王涯 - Refers to Wang Yá, the Chancellor of the Yongning Prince.
  • 三怪 - Means 'three strange occurrences.'
  • 淅米匠人苏润 - 'Ximi' is a type of rice, and '匠人' refers to a craftsman or artisan. '苏润' is the name of the person.
  • 王家咎征 - '咎征' means ominous signs or bad omens associated with the Wang family.
  • 宅南有一井 - Refers to a well located to the south of the Wang family's residence.
  • 每夜常沸涌有声 - Describes the well bubbling and making noise every night.
  • 昼窥之 - Means 'to look at it during the day.'
  • 或见铜（一作叵）厮罗，或见银熨斗者 - '铜（一作叵）厮罗' and '银熨斗者' are unclear phrases, possibly referring to strange apparitions or objects seen in the well. '叵' is an alternative reading for '铜.'
  • 水腐不可饮 - Means 'the water has spoiled and is undrinkable.'
  • 禅床 - A meditation bench or seat.
  • 柘材丝绳 - '柘材' refers to material from the Chinese privet tree, and '丝绳' means silk rope, indicating the craftsmanship of the bench.
  • 各聚一处 - Means 'gathered in one place.'
  • 王甚恶之 - Means 'Wang Yá greatly disliked it.'
  • 命焚于灶下 - Means 'ordered to be burned in the stove.'
  • 长子孟博 - Refers to Wang Yá's eldest son, Mengbo.
  • 晨兴，见堂地上有凝血数滴 - Means 'in the morning, he saw several drops of congealed blood on the floor of the hall.'
  • 踪至大门方绝 - Means 'the trail ended at the main gate.'
  • 遽令铲去 - Means 'immediately ordered it to be scraped away.'
  • 许州有一老僧 - Refers to an elderly monk in Xu Prefecture.
  • 每寐熟即喉声如鼓簧 - Means 'every time he slept deeply, his throat sounded like a drum or a reed flute.'
  • 若成韵节 - Means 'if it formed a rhythm.'
  • 许州伶人 - Refers to musicians or performers in Xu Prefecture.
  • 伺其寝，即谱其声 - Means 'they waited for him to sleep and then transcribed his sound.'
  • 按之丝竹 - Means 'played it on string and wind instruments.'
  • 皆合古奏 - Means 'all matched ancient music.'
  • 僧觉，亦不自知 - Means 'the monk woke up, but did not know it.'
  • 魏溪 - Refers to a man named Wei Xi.
  • 好食白鱼 - Means 'enjoys eating white fish.'
  • 日命仆市之 - Means 'every day he ordered his servant to buy it.'
  • 或不获，辄笞责 - Means 'if not obtained, he would whip and scold.'
  • 猎者绐之曰 - Means 'a hunter deceived him by saying.'
  • 某向打鱼，网得一麝 - Means 'I was fishing and caught a musk deer.'
  • 因渔而获，不亦异乎 - Means 'to catch something while fishing, isn't that strange?'
  • 具事于溪 - Means 'reported it to Wei Xi.'
  • 审如是，或有灵矣 - Means 'if it is indeed so, perhaps it has some sort of spirit.'
  • 因置诸榻，日夕荐香火 - Means 'so he placed it on the bed and offered incense and fire every day.'
  • 历数年不坏 - Means 'for several years it did not decay.'
  • 颇有吉凶之验 - Means 'it often showed signs of good or bad fortune.'
  • 溪友人恶溪所为 - Means 'Wei Xi's friend disliked his actions.'
  • 伺其出，烹而食之 - Means 'waited for him to leave and then cooked and ate it.'
  • 亦无其灵 - Means 'it also lost its spirit.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 永宁王相王涯 - Wang Yá was a prominent official during the Tang Dynasty, known for his role as Chancellor.
  • 三怪 - Refers to three unusual events or omens associated with Wang Yá's household.
  • 许州 - Refers to Xu Prefecture, a historical region in present-day Henan Province.
  • 魏溪 - A historical figure, possibly a local official or wealthy individual.
  • 猎者 - Refers to hunters, who were often seen as knowledgeable about local folklore and superstitions.
  • 麝 - A musk deer, often associated with rarity and value in Chinese culture.
  • 禅床 - A meditation bench, often used in Buddhist practice.
  • 丝竹 - Refers to string and wind instruments, commonly used in classical Chinese music.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 饮彻 - After drinking has ended or once the feast is over.
  • 命引进妓数四 - Ordered to bring in several courtesans, where '数四' indicates 'several' or 'a few'.
  • 支鬟撩鬓 - Describes the courtesans arranging their hair, '支鬟' means 'to support the bun,' and '撩鬓' means 'to flick the bangs'.
  • 缥若神仙 - Seemingly like immortals, describing the courtesans' ethereal appearance.
  • 其舞杯闪球之令 - Refers to a dance involving cups and balls, '闪球' means 'to flick the ball'.
  • 悉新而多思 - All new and full of thought, indicating the creativity and originality of the performances.
  • 有金器容数升 - There was a golden vessel capable of holding several sheng, where '数升' indicates 'several sheng' (a unit of volume).
  • 云擎鲸口 - Said to hold the mouth of a whale, describing the size and grandeur of the vessel.
  • 钿以珠粒 - Inlaid with pearls, describing the decoration of the vessel.
  • 豪家子不识 - The young man from a wealthy family did not recognize it.
  • 予暂有所适 - I will be away for a moment, indicating a temporary departure.
  • 揖客而退 - Bowed to the guests and withdrew, describing the host's polite gesture.
  • 骑从如州牧 - Accompanied by attendants like a state governor, indicating the host's high status.
  • 列烛而出 - Exited with lit torches, describing the grand exit.
  • 无废欢笑 - Do not let the merriment cease, encouraging the guests to continue enjoying themselves.
  • 饮既阑 - The drinking had already ended, indicating the conclusion of the banquet.
  • 自持锸开东墙一穴 - Took a spade and dug a hole in the eastern wall, describing the courtesans' actions.
  • 推豪家子于墙外 - Pushed the young man out of the wall, indicating the method of his escape.
  • 遂乞食，方达蜀 - He then begged for food and eventually reached Shu, describing his journey back to his home.
  • 其家失已多年 - His family had lost him for many years, indicating the long separation.
  • 意其异物 - Thought he was a strange creature, describing the family's initial reaction.
  • 道其初始信 - Told of his origin and they believed him, indicating the family's eventual acceptance.
  • 贞元初事 - This happened at the beginning of the Zhenyuan era, providing the historical context.

  • 往往应于未兆 - Means 'often responds to things before they become evident or manifest.'
  • 勤勤若相别为赠之意 - Means 'repeatedly and earnestly, as if giving a parting gift.'
  • 监军院宴满川等为戏 - Means 'the military governor's office hosted a banquet for Manchuan et al. as a form of entertainment.'
  • 以求衣粮 - Means 'to obtain clothing and food.'
  • 少师李相怒 - Means 'the junior tutor Li was angry.'

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 凡死者肌骨未复于土 - Literally means 'all those who have died, their flesh and bones not yet returned to the earth,' referring to the process of decomposition and burial.
  • 魂神不为阴司所籍 - Means 'their souls and spirits are not registered by the underworld,' indicating that the deceased's spiritual status is unsettled.
  • 离散恍惚，如梦如醉 - Describes a state of being scattered and confused, like a dream or a drunken stupor.
  • 君或留念幽魂 - Means 'if you, sir, retain any remembrance of the wandering spirit,' suggesting a lingering connection or memory.
  • 亦是阴德 - Means 'it is also an act of merit in the underworld,' implying that such remembrance has moral or spiritual value.
  • 使妾遗骸得归泉壤 - Means 'to allow my remains to return to the grave,' expressing a wish for proper burial.
  • 精爽有托 - Means 'to have a place for my spirit to rest,' indicating a desire for a peaceful afterlife.
  • 某生业素薄 - Means 'my means of livelihood are usually modest,' referring to a low social or economic status.
  • 力且不办 - Means 'I lack the strength to accomplish it,' indicating an inability to take action.
  • 某虽为鬼，不废女工 - Means 'although I am a ghost, I do not abandon needlework,' suggesting that even in death, the woman continues her work.
  • 自安此，常造雨衣 - Means 'since settling here, I often make raincoats,' describing the woman's occupation.
  • 与胡氏家佣作 - Means 'I work for the family of Hu,' indicating that she works as a servant or laborer.
  • 凡数岁矣 - Means 'for several years,' indicating the duration of her work.
  • 所聚十三万 - Means 'the amount I have accumulated is thirteen thousand,' referring to the money she has saved.
  • 备掩藏固有余也 - Means 'it is more than enough for burial,' indicating that the money is sufficient for her burial.
  • 具以告 - Means 'to tell him in detail,' indicating a full explanation.
  • 即与偕往殡所 - Means 'and went with him to the burial place,' describing the action taken.
  • 毁瘗视之 - Means 'to destroy the grave and inspect it,' indicating an examination of the burial site.
  • 散钱培榇 - Means 'to scatter money and fill the coffin,' describing the act of placing money in the coffin.
  • 缗之数如言 - Means 'the amount of money matches what was said,' indicating that the amount is as expected.
  • 胡氏与郝哀而异之 - Means 'Hu and Hao mourned and found it strange,' indicating their emotional reaction.
  • 复率钱与同辈合二十万 - Means 'they then gathered money with their peers totaling twenty thousand,' indicating a collective effort.
  • 盛其凶仪 - Means 'to prepare the funeral rites,' describing the preparations for the funeral.
  • 瘗于鹿顶原 - Means 'to bury her at Lutopeng Plain,' indicating the location of the burial.
  • 其夕，见梦于胡、郝 - Means 'that night, she appeared in a dream to Hu and Hao,' indicating a supernatural event.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 阴司 - Refers to the underworld, a concept in Chinese folklore and religion.
  • 泉壤 - Refers to the grave or the underworld, often used metaphorically.
  • 女工 - Refers to needlework or sewing, a common occupation for women in traditional Chinese society.
  • 雨衣 - Refers to raincoats, indicating the type of clothing the woman makes.
  • 殡所 - Refers to the burial place or cemetery.
  • 凶仪 - Refers to funeral rites or ceremonies.
  • 鹿顶原 - Refers to Lutopeng Plain, a specific location for the burial.

Proper Nouns

  • 郝 - The name of the man who is approached for help.
  • 胡氏 - The family for whom the woman works.
  • 鹿顶原 - The location where the woman is buried.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 扪萝垂踵 - Climbing vines and treading on the ground; describes the act of climbing or exploring the terrain.
  • 无幽不迹 - Leaving no hidden place unexplored; indicates thorough exploration.

  • 旋绕绳床 - Spinning around the rope bed; describes a performance or trick.

  • 蓬转涡急 - Spinning like a whirlwind; describes a rapid, spinning motion.
  • 但睹衣色成规 - Only seeing the color of the clothes forming a circle; indicates the visual effect of the performance.
  • 攸忽失所 - Suddenly lost; indicates the disappearance of the performer.

  • 长庆中 - The Longqing period of the Tang Dynasty (821-824 AD).
  • 头陀 - A wandering monk, often associated with ascetic practices.
  • 悟空 - A name that could be associated with the famous monk Xuanzang (Tripitaka) from the *Journey to the West*.
  • 道士 - A Taoist priest, often associated with spiritual practices and magic.
  • 绳床 - A rope bed, a type of simple bed used by monks and Taoist priests.
  • 木枭枝 - Wooden branches, possibly used in a performance or trick.
  • 盖危石 - Covering dangerous rocks, possibly part of a performance.
  • 猿悬鸟 - Describes the agility and speed of the performance, likening it to a monkey or bird.

  • 朱陵洞 - The Zhuling Cave.
  • 长庆中 - The Longqing period of the Tang Dynasty.

  • 悟空 - The name of the monk.

  • 弹棋起自魏室 - Literally 'The game of *danqi* originated in the Wei court,' where *danqi* is a traditional Chinese board game.
  • 妆奁戏也 - Literally 'a game in the makeup box,' referring to a game often played by women.
  • 《典论》云 - A reference to *Lunheng* (典论), a work by Cao Pi, a prince of the Wei Kingdom.
  • 予于他戏弄之事，少所喜 - Literally 'I have little interest in other playful activities,' where '予' is a first-person pronoun.
  • 唯弹棋略尽其巧 - Literally 'only in *danqi* have I mastered its intricacies.'
  • 京师有马合乡侯、东方世安、张公子 - Refers to specific individuals in the capital, with titles indicating their status.
  • 恨不与数子对 - Literally 'I regret not being able to play against these few,' expressing a desire to play against them.
  • 不起于魏室明矣 - Literally 'it is clear that it did not originate in the Wei court,' but the meaning is more nuanced.
  • 今弹棋用棋二十四，以色别贵贱 - Literally 'today, *danqi* uses twenty-four pieces, distinguished by color to signify rank.'
  • 棋绝后一豆 - Literally 'after the game ends, one bean is placed,' referring to a ritual or marker.
  • 《座右方》云 - A reference to *Zuo You Fang* (座右方), a book of advice or rules.
  • 白黑各六棋，依六博棋形 - Literally 'six white and six black pieces, arranged in the shape of a *liubo* board,' where *liubo* is an ancient board game.
  • 颇似枕状 - Literally 'quite like a pillow,' describing the shape of the pieces.
  • 魏戏法 - Refers to the rules or methods of playing *danqi* during the Wei period.
  • 先立一棋于局中，斗余者，思白黑围绕之 - Literally 'first, place one piece in the center of the board, then the remaining players think about surrounding the white and black pieces.'
  • 十八筹成都 - Literally 'eighteen sticks make up a city,' referring to a scoring or completion condition.
  • 《梁职仪》曰 - A reference to *Nan Liang Zhiyi* (梁职仪), a book of official etiquette during the Southern Liang Dynasty.
  • 八座尚书以紫纱裹手版，垂白丝于首如笔 - Literally 'the eight-seat Shangshu (ministers) wrap their handboards with purple gauze and hang white silk at the top like a pen.'
  • 《通志》曰 - A reference to *Tongzhi* (通志), a comprehensive historical work.
  • 今录仆射、尚书手版，以紫皮裹之，名曰笏 - Literally 'today, the handboards of the Sheshi (Counsellors) and Shangshu (ministers) are wrapped in purple leather, called *hu*.'
  • 梁中世已来，唯八座尚书执笏者，白笔缀头，以紫纱囊之 - Literally 'since the middle of the Liang Dynasty, only the eight-seat Shangshu who hold the *hu* have white pens attached to the top, wrapped in purple gauze.'
  • 其余公卿但执手版 - Literally 'the other officials only hold handboards.'
  • 今人相传云 - Literally 'people today say.'
  • 陈希烈不便税笏，骑马以帛裹，令左右执之 - Literally 'Chen Xilie found it inconvenient to tax the *hu*, so he wrapped it in silk when riding a horse and had his attendants hold it.'
  • 李右座见云 - Literally 'Li Right Seat said.'
  • 便为将来故事，甚失之矣 - Literally 'it became a future precedent, which was very wrong.'
  • 今人谓丑为貌寝，误矣 - Literally 'people today call ugliness 'maoqin,' which is a mistake.'
  • 《魏志》曰 - A reference to *Wei Zhi* (魏志), a historical record of the Wei Kingdom.
  • 刘表以王粲貌侵，体通说，不甚重之 - Literally 'Liu Biao, because of Wang Can's appearance, thought he was too ordinary, and did not highly regard him.'
  • 一云“貌寝，体通说，甚重之” - Literally 'one says 'his appearance was ordinary, but he was very knowledgeable, and he was highly regarded.'
  • 注云：“侵，貌不足也” - Literally 'the note says, 'qin means appearance is insufficient.'

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 各征象事须切 - Emphasizes the need for precision and pertinence in representation.

  • 一宝之数，无钩不可 - Metaphorical expression where '一宝之数' refers to a single treasure or valuable thing, and '无钩不可' suggests that without a means of retrieval or connection, it cannot be obtained.
  • 唯猊可伏，非驼所堪 - '唯猊可伏' means 'only the lion can be tamed,' and '非驼所堪' means 'not something a camel can endure.'

  • 与马同渡，负猿而行 - '与马同渡' means 'crossing the river with a horse,' and '负猿而行' means 'carrying a monkey while walking.'
  • 色青力劣，名香几重 - '色青力劣' means 'green in color and weak in strength,' and '名香几重' means 'how many layers of fragrance does it have.'
  • 尾既出牖，身可取兴 - '尾既出牖' means 'the tail has already come out of the window,' and '身可取兴' means 'the body can be taken as an example.'
  • 六牙生花，七支拄地 - '六牙生花' means 'six tusks have flowers,' and '七支拄地' means 'seven branches support the ground.'
  • 形如珂雪，力绝羁琐 - '形如珂雪' means 'shape like jade snow,' and '力绝羁琐' means 'strength surpasses trivialities.'
  • 园开胁上，河出鼻中 - '园开胁上' means 'garden opens on the side,' and '河出鼻中' means 'river flows out of the nose.'
  • 一醉难调，六对曾胜 - '一醉难调' means 'one drunkenness is hard to regulate,' and '六对曾胜' means 'six pairs have been victorious.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 各征象事须切，不得引俗书 - Emphasizes the importance of precision and the avoidance of common or less authoritative sources, reflecting a scholarly approach to documentation and citation.
  • 一宝之数，无钩不可 - The metaphor of a treasure without a hook suggests the importance of having a means to access or utilize valuable knowledge or resources.
  • 唯猊可伏，非驼所堪 - The metaphor of a lion being tamed and a camel not enduring certain conditions highlights the differences in capabilities and resilience among different entities.
  • 坑中无底，迹中无胜 - The metaphor of a bottomless pit and no victory in traces suggests the futility of certain pursuits or the lack of clear outcomes in certain situations.
  • 与马同渡，负猿而行 - The metaphor of crossing a river with a horse and carrying a monkey while walking illustrates the balance and coordination required in complex tasks.
  • 色青力劣，名香几重 - The description of something being green in color and weak in strength, yet having multiple layers of fragrance, suggests a contrast between appearance and substance.
  • 尾既出牖，身可取兴 - The metaphor of a tail coming out of a window and the body being taken as an example suggests the visibility and influence of certain actions or behaviors.
  • 六牙生花，七支拄地 - The metaphor of six tusks having flowers and seven branches supporting the ground suggests the complexity and interconnectedness of certain structures or systems.
  • 形如珂雪，力绝羁琐 - The description of a shape like jade snow and strength surpassing trivialities suggests the elegance and power of certain qualities.
  • 园开胁上，河出鼻中 - The metaphor of a garden opening on the side and a river flowing out of the nose suggests the unexpected and unusual nature of certain phenomena.
  • 一醉难调，六对曾胜 - The metaphor of one drunkenness being hard to regulate and six pairs having been victorious suggests the challenges and successes in managing complex situations.
